rubric: Lists at least 5 named self-evolving search systems or frameworks (e.g. SE-Search, EvolveSearch, ASL, SSP, ConvSearch-R1)
Identifies at least 4 distinct evolution mechanisms (e.g. RL-based, memory-driven, self-play, feedback-loop, knowledge distillation)
Names at least 3 academic papers published in 2025 or 2026 on self-evolving search or retrieval
Mentions at least 2 open-source GitHub repositories for self-evolving search with star counts or links
Covers Chinese community perspectives or Chinese-language implementations of self-evolving search
Includes at least 2 benchmark datasets used to evaluate self-evolving search systems (e.g. BEIR, TREC, HotpotQA, FRAMES)
Names at least 3 research institutions or labs active in self-evolving search (e.g. universities, AI labs)
Describes the role of reinforcement learning (RL) in at least one self-evolving search system with specific technique names
Covers memory-based evolution: how systems accumulate and reuse past search trajectories or strategies
Mentions self-play co-evolution where an LLM acts as both proposer and solver in a search context
Covers at least one commercial or product application of adaptive/self-evolving search beyond academic prototypes
Includes a timeline covering key milestones from 2023 through 2026 in self-evolving search development
Names at least 2 specific quantitative performance improvements reported by self-evolving systems (e.g. % gain on benchmark)
Mentions RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) systems that incorporate self-evolving or adaptive retrieval
Covers agent-based search systems where the search agent evolves its own strategy (not just the model weights)
Provides a conceptual taxonomy or framework organizing self-evolving search approaches by mechanism or architecture
Compares at least 3 evolution mechanisms head-to-head with at least one concrete tradeoff stated for each
Identifies a directional trend in the field (e.g. shift from offline to online learning, from keyword to neural, from single to multi-agent)
Explains WHY a specific approach succeeded or failed with a causal mechanism, not just correlation
States a recommendation for which self-evolving search approach fits which use case (e.g. enterprise vs research vs real-time)
Identifies at least 2 known failure modes or limitations of current self-evolving search systems
Identifies at least one open research problem or unresolved controversy in self-evolving search
Discusses the gap between academic self-evolving search research and production deployment realities
Every major claim, system name, or paper reference is supported by at least one citation from search results
Includes an explicit gap declaration section stating what was NOT found or remains uncertain
